statement: EU membership would not create new free movement rights for construction workers beyond what already exists under the EEA Agreement. Iceland already has full access to EEA labour mobility, and the construction sector already draws heavily on Polish, Lithuanian, and Portuguese workers. However, EU membership could affect labour supply indirectly: (1) full participation in the European Labour Authority (ELA) could improve cross-border worker coordination and reduce administrative friction; (2) enhanced mutual recognition of professional qualifications under EU frameworks could simplify the process for skilled tradespeople from EU countries to work in Iceland; (3) access to EU-funded vocational training programmes (e.g. Erasmus+, which Iceland already partially participates in through the EEA) could help address domestic skills gaps in the construction trades.
source_name: European Labour Authority — Annual Report 2024; EU Directive 2005/36/EC on professional qualifications
caveats: The practical impact on labour supply would likely be marginal, since EEA free movement already provides the fundamental right. The main barriers to attracting construction workers to Iceland are housing costs for workers themselves, Iceland's remote location, and the króna's volatility — none of which EU membership directly resolves. Conversely, some argue that EU membership could increase outward migration of Icelandic workers, though EEA rights already enable this.

related_entries.statement: Iceland already participates in the free movement of workers through the EEA Agreement, which has been in force since 1994. EEA nationals have the right to live and work in Iceland without work permits, and Icelanders have reciprocal rights across the EU/EEA. EU membership would not create new free movement rights beyond what already exists under the EEA framework. However, EU membership would give Iceland voting rights in shaping the rules governing free movement, whereas under the EEA Iceland implements EU single market legislation without a formal vote in the Council or European Parliament.

The Icelandic construction sector employs approximately 15,000–17,000 workers, representing roughly 8% of the total workforce. Foreign nationals account for an estimated 40–50% of construction workers, predominantly from Poland, Lithuania, and Portugal. The sector faces chronic skills shortages in specialised trades such as electricians, plumbers, and HVAC technicians. The Icelandic construction industry association (Samtök iðnaðarins) has repeatedly highlighted that domestic vocational training produces far fewer skilled tradespeople than the industry requires — approximately 200–300 certified journeymen per year against an estimated need of 500+. The sector's dependence on EEA migrant labour is already substantial under the current EEA framework.

As of 2025, approximately 70% of EU internal market legislation has been incorporated into Icelandic law through the EEA Agreement. This covers free movement of goods, services, capital, and persons, but excludes fisheries, agriculture, and justice/home affairs.

claims.claim_text: Það er óvéfengjanlega ósatt að ESB-aðild myndi sjálfkrafa leiða til þess að erlend skip með erlendar áhafnir komi og leggi sjávarplássin í eyði.
